---

# Translate CLI/TUI — how to make it feel native in this dotfiles environment

## Bottom line (the recipe)
A new `translate` tool should be a **Python single-file launcher using PEP 723 inline metadata run via `uv`**, using **tyro** (CLI) + **rich** (output) + **textual** (if TUI) + **platformdirs** (paths), placed at `dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_translate` with shared logic in a real package at `scripts/translate/`. Config in **`~/.config/translate/config.toml`** (TOML), history data in **`~/.local/share/translate/`** (sqlite), plus a **`tv translate` channel** for fuzzy history selection. This exactly mirrors the existing `yth` tool, which is the closest analog to "searchable translation history."

---

## 1. Existing custom in-house CLIs

Source lives in `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_*`, deployed by chezmoi to `~/.dotfiles/bin/` (mode 0755, first on PATH). Two languages only: **Python (PEP723/uv)** for anything nontrivial, **bash** for simple cross-platform utilities.

| CLI | Lang | Structure | CLI framework | TUI / interactive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **fleet** | Python (PEP723 uv) | Thin launcher + `scripts/fleet/*.py` (apply/tmux/info/pueue/exec/hosts) | **tyro** + rich + asyncssh | `fleet hosts` shells to `tv fleet-hosts` picker |
| **mlf** | Python (PEP723 uv) | Launcher + `scripts/mlf/*.py`, shared helpers in `scripts/mlf/__init__.py` | **tyro** + rich + plotext + **textual** | **Yes — bare `mlf` launches a Textual TUI dashboard**; `mlf tv` opens `tv mlflow` |
| **yth** | Python (PEP723 uv) | Launcher + `scripts/yth/*.py`, shared in `__init__.py` | **tyro** + rich + yt-dlp + platformdirs | **Bare `yth` execs `tv yth` picker**; sqlite FTS5 search |
| **pqsum** | Python (PEP723 uv) | Single 44k file | **argparse** (the outlier — not tyro) + rich | No |
| **mi-router** | Python (PEP723 uv) | Single file | **tyro** + httpx + rich + platformdirs | interactive getpass prompt |
| **x** | **bash** | single file | hand-rolled `usage()` | No (clipboard util, cross-platform fallbacks) |
| **ping-monitor** | **bash** | single file | hand-rolled | No |
| reyee / sms | Python (PEP723 uv) | single file | tyro | read-only router CLIs |

**The canonical launcher shebang + dependency block** (from `yth`, `mlf`, `fleet`, `pqsum`, `mi-router` — all identical form):
```python
#!/usr/bin/env -S uv run --quiet --script
# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = [
#   "tyro>=0.9",
#   "rich>=13.9",
#   "platformdirs>=4",
# ]
# ///
```
No virtualenv, no install step — `uv` resolves deps on first run. **Python 3.11+** everywhere.

**Structure pattern (for anything with >1 subcommand):** thin dispatcher launcher in `dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_<name>` (holds the PEP723 deps + USAGE + argv routing) + a real Python package in `scripts/<name>/` with `__init__.py` holding shared helpers and one module per subcommand. The launcher discovers the scripts dir **at runtime via `chezmoi source-path`** (falls back to `~/.local/share/chezmoi`) so it works from any cwd. `mlf` and `yth` are the reference implementations; CLAUDE.md explicitly says "shared helpers live in `scripts/<tool>/__init__.py` — refactor there, not in the call sites."

---

## 2. XDG config conventions

Strongly XDG-first (docs: `docs/tools/xdg.md`, `docs/this_repo/config-conventions.md`). Rule: "prefer XDG to keep `$HOME` tidy."

- **Config → `~/.config/<tool>/config.toml`** — TOML is the preferred format. Read via `tomllib` and/or `platformdirs.user_config_dir`. Examples: `yth` uses `user_config_dir/yth/config.toml`; `mi-router` reads `~/.config/mi-router/config.toml`.
- **Data (irreplaceable) → `~/.local/share/<tool>/`** — e.g. `yth` sqlite DB at `platformdirs.user_data_dir/yth/history.db`.
- **Cache → `$XDG_CACHE_HOME/<tool>/`** — e.g. `pqsum` caches to `$XDG_CACHE_HOME/pqsum/<host>-<mode>.json`.
- **Config-resolution precedence** (idiomatic here, from `mi-router`): CLI flags → `~/.config/<tool>/config.toml` → env vars → interactive prompt (TTY only, offers to save).
- **Secrets:** never tracked; gitignored/chezmoi-ignored files sourced presence-gated, e.g. `~/.config/zsh/secrets.zsh`. For a translate API key: `~/.config/translate/config.toml` (chezmoi-ignored) or an env var pulled from the existing secrets file.

Format preference overall: **TOML for tool config**, YAML only for ansible, JSON reserved for machine-readable schemas/`--json` output.

---

## 3. How a new CLI gets on PATH & managed

**PATH mechanism:** `dot_config/shell/00_exports.sh.tmpl` line 26 prepends `~/.dotfiles/bin` first:
```
export PATH="$HOME/.dotfiles/bin:$HOME/bin:$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"
```
So: drop `executable_translate` into `dot_dotfiles/bin/`, run `chezmoi apply`, and it's live from any cwd. **No `just` recipe or symlink needed** to register it (there are optional `just fleet`/`just mlf` passthrough recipes purely for dev convenience).

**If it should also be a standalone uv-tool binary** (not just a self-booting launcher), add an entry to `dot_ansible/roles/python_uv_tools/defaults/main.yml` (`- name: <pkg>` / `binary: <exe>`). This is the "dual pattern" CLAUDE.md describes for `mlf`↔`mlflow` and `yth`↔`yt-dlp`: deps declared in BOTH the launcher's PEP723 block (self-boot) AND `python_uv_tools` (standalone). For a from-scratch translate tool you likely only need the PEP723 launcher.

**CLAUDE.md cross-file "mirror" rules** triggered by adding a new `executable_*` CLI (`/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/CLAUDE.md`, rows 20–26):
- Tab completion in **two files** (kept in sync): `dot_config/zsh/tools/<NN>_translate_completion.zsh` + `dot_config/bash/<NN>_translate_completion.bash` (see `53_yth_completion.*` as the template; hand-written "Strategy B" like fleet/mlf/pqsum/x, or tyro-self-gen "Strategy A" like mi-router).
- `docs/tools/translate.md` reference page.
- Optional `tv` channel `dot_config/television/cable/translate.toml`.
- Update `docs/this_repo/tool-managers.md` A–Z index and `docs/shells/aliases.md` if it ships a completion helper.
- If surfaced to agents, mention in `dot_agents/skills/chezmoi-dotfiles/SKILL.md.tmpl` (re-renders each apply).

---

## 4. Television (tv) — ideal for translation-history fuzzy selection

~40+ custom channels in `dot_config/television/cable/*.toml`; matcher is **nucleo** (same as Helix). **`yth.toml` is a near-perfect template for a translation-history picker.** Its shape:
- `[source] command = "yth list --tsv"` emitting TSV; `display`/`output` use `{split:\t:N}` field extraction; `no_sort = true` because the list is already ordered temporally (the doc's rule #5: preserve order for temporal data).
- `[preview] command = "yth show '{...}' | bat ..."` (DB-only, instant).
- `[actions]` with `mode = "fork"`/`"execute"`; keybindings use **`Alt+`** letters because the tmux prefix is `Ctrl+b` (a `ctrl-b` binding never reaches tv).
- Bare `yth` (no args) execs `tv yth`.

**Important design constraint:** tv's source is single-shot and never sees the live query, so the channel can only fuzzy-match the display line (metadata). Deep/full-text search must be a **CLI subcommand** — that's why `yth search --subs` exists (sqlite FTS5) alongside the `tv yth` picker. A translate tool should do the same: `tv translate` picks over recent history by display line; `translate search <query>` does FTS over stored translations.

So the native pattern is: `translate list --tsv` → `tv translate` channel; bare `translate` execs `tv translate`; `translate search` for real text search.

---

## 5. fzf / gum usage (division of labor)

Doc: `docs/tools/tv-vs-fzf.md`. Both are used, with clear lanes:
- **fzf** owns shell-native integration: `Ctrl+R` (history/atuin), `Ctrl+T` (files), `Alt+C` (cd), `**<TAB>` completion. Config: `dot_config/zsh/tools/10_fzf.zsh`.
- **tv** owns structured domain pickers (sesh, tools, aliases, yth, mlflow) — declarative TOML channels. **This is the recommended path for a new domain picker like translation history.**
- **gum** is the idiom for interactive prompts *inside shell functions* (not fuzzy list-picking): e.g. `dot_config/shell/29_media.sh` `media-pick` uses `gum file` / `gum choose` / `gum input`; `dot_config/shell/55_macos_mem.sh` uses `gum confirm`.

For the translate tool: use **tv** for selecting from history; use **textual** (like `mlf`) if you want a full interactive TUI; use **gum** only if you add a quick shell-function wrapper needing a confirm/input prompt.

---

## 6. Existing translation tooling — NONE

A clean search across `dot_config`, `dot_dotfiles`, `dot_ansible`, and the aliases doc found **no `trans`, `translate`, DeepL, Google Translate, or translate-shell CLI/alias/function.** The only adjacent hits:
- A comment in `dot_config/homebrew/Brewfile.darwin.tmpl` mentioning the "Immersive Translate" **browser extension** (not a CLI).
- `dot_config/zsh/tools/05_aisuggest.zsh` — an AI ZLE widget (trigger `Alt+;`) that "translates" natural-language → a shell command (LLM-backed; unrelated to language translation, but worth knowing as prior art for an inline AI-powered widget).
- mkdocs i18n (`*.zh-TW.md`) doc translations — build tooling, not a translate CLI.

So this is greenfield; no naming/behavior collisions.

---

## 7. Config/secrets format + lint/format/test conventions

- **Config format:** TOML (via `tomllib`), stored under `~/.config/<tool>/`. Secrets: chezmoi-ignored file sourced presence-gated, or env var.
- **Python lint:** `.ruff_cache/` (ruff 0.15.20) exists at repo root, so **ruff is run ad-hoc** (editor/CLI, default rules) — but note it is **NOT wired into pre-commit and there is no `[tool.ruff]` config**; `pyproject.toml` only defines the mkdocs docs-build env, not these scripts. A root `pyrightconfig.json` exists for type-checking. `requires-python = ">=3.11"` is the standard.
- **Shell lint/format (enforced in `.pre-commit-config.yaml`):** shellcheck (`--severity=warning` for `scripts/*.sh`, `--severity=error` for `dot_config/{shell,bash}/`) + shfmt (`-i 2 -ci -bn -w`, i.e. 2-space indent). `.tmpl` and zsh files are excluded.
- **Other pre-commit hooks:** gitleaks + detect-private-key + a secret-redactor, standard whitespace/EOF/large-file/merge-conflict, check-toml/json/yaml, actionlint, and a prompt-generation sync check.
- **Tests:** `just test` = docker-test; bats tests exist; `just check` = `lint` + `chezmoi-dry-run`; `just check-all` = `lint bats docker-test`; `just lint` = ansible-syntax-check + pre-commit-run-all.

---

## Key file references
- Launcher templates: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_dotfiles/bin/executable_yth`, `.../executable_mlf`, `.../executable_fleet`
- Shared-package pattern: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/yth/` (`__init__.py` + per-subcommand modules), `scripts/mlf/`
- tv channel template: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/television/cable/yth.toml`
- Config conventions: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/docs/this_repo/config-conventions.md`, `docs/tools/xdg.md`
- PATH: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/shell/00_exports.sh.tmpl` (line 26)
- uv-tool declaration: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_ansible/roles/python_uv_tools/defaults/main.yml`
- Cross-file maintenance rules for new CLIs: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/CLAUDE.md` (rows 20–26)
- fzf vs tv guidance: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/docs/tools/tv-vs-fzf.md`
- Completion template: `/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/dot_config/zsh/tools/53_yth_completion.zsh`
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-09 11:14:38Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="generic" data-tool-name="AskUserQuestion"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **AskUserQuestion**</summary>
**Which language/framework should the CLI+TUI use? Your existing custom CLIs (yth, mlf, fleet, mi-router) are all Python+uv+tyro+rich, and mlf already ships a Textual TUI — so Python maximizes consistency and reuse. The sub-choice is the interactive layer.**

Options:
- **Python + Textual** - Matches all your existing CLIs. Full interactive TUI (panes, history sidebar, live), rich CLI output, async. Most batteries-included; heaviest.
- **Python + prompt_toolkit** - Lighter REPL-style loop; snappiest type-and-translate feel, minimal chrome. Still Python+uv+tyro+rich for the CLI half.
- **Go + Bubble Tea** - Single static binary, very snappy, matches your Charm tooling (gum/glow/vhs/tv). But a new language for your custom-CLI set.
- **Rust + ratatui** - Fastest, single binary. Most implementation effort; immediate-mode UI. Least consistent with your Python CLI set.

**What should the DEFAULT translation engine be? (All can be configured/switched; this is just the out-of-box default.)**

Options:
- **Auto fallback chain** - Try copilot-proxy (best models) → Ollama (local) → free Google API. Most robust; always works even if proxy is down. Recommended.
- **copilot-proxy only** - claude-sonnet-5 via localhost:4141. Best quality, already running, no API key. Note: Copilot ToS discourages non-GitHub agents.
- **Ollama (local)** - llama3.2:3b, fully private/offline, no ToS concern. Weaker translation quality, only model currently pulled.
- **Free Google API** - Fast, no key, no LLM. Plain translation only — no grammar notes/alternatives/explanations.

**Which engine types should we actually BUILD in this first iteration? (You listed all three as in-scope; multi-select what's must-have for v1 vs. deferred.)** _(select multiple)_

Options:
- **LLM backend** - OpenAI-compatible client covering copilot-proxy, Ollama, OpenRouter, LiteLLM, generic base_url+key. Built-in prompts + structured output. The core.
- **Free translate API** - Google (+ optionally Bing) unofficial endpoints. No key, fast plain translation + language detection.
- **Dictionary lookup** - Word/phrase definitions via public dictionaries (dictionaryapi.dev / Wiktionary), exact match → fuzzy fallback.
- **History + memory** - sqlite-FTS history, tv picker, remembers last language pair + source auto-detect/fixed-target. (Recommended for v1.)

**How should the interactive TUI trigger inference? You said 'user typing stop, then infer ASAP' — which gesture do you mean?**

Options:
- **Enter = translate** - Enter submits immediately; Shift/Alt+Enter inserts a newline for multi-line input. Fast and conventional.
- **Live debounce** - Translates automatically ~400ms after you stop typing. Truest to 'when the user stops'. Costs more LLM calls.
- **Explicit submit key** - Type freely (multi-line), then Ctrl+Enter / Ctrl+D to run. Full control, never fires early.
- **Literal 'stop' sentinel** - Typing the word 'stop' on its own line triggers inference. Literal reading of your request.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The user went with Go and the Charm Tea stack (Bubble Tea, Lip Gloss, and cobra) for a single static binary—skipping the typical Python conventions in favor of their existing Charm ecosystem. They're setting up an auto-fallback chain for the default engine starting with copilot-proxy.

For v1, they want all engines: LLM backend, free translation API, dictionary lookup, and history with memory. The submit gesture will use live debounce (~400ms) by default but also support immediate translation on Enter, with debounce being toggleable.

Now I'm thinking through the Go ecosystem choices—cobra for CLI, and for config I'm weighing viper versus a lighter approach with BurntSushi's TOML library. The tricky part is respecting XDG paths properly across platforms, since `os.UserConfigDir()` returns different defaults on macOS versus Linux, so I might need the adrg/xdg library to honor `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` consistently.

For the TUI stack, I'm using Bubble Tea with Lip Gloss for styling and Bubbles for components like textarea and textinput. For HTTP calls to OpenAI-compatible and Google endpoints, stdlib `net/http` works fine with SSE streaming for the LLM. For SQLite history, I'm leaning toward modernc.org/sqlite since it's pure Go with no CGO, making static binaries and cross-compilation easier, and it includes FTS5 support built-in.

For language detection offline, I'll use whatlanggo since it's lightweight, though the LLM path can detect via prompt or rely on backend detection. For fuzzy matching on language names and codes, I'll pick one of the standard Go fuzzy libraries like sahilm/fuzzy or agnivade/levenshtein.

The interactive TUI will use Bubble Tea's timer mechanism to debounce input—firing a translate command 400ms after the last keystroke, with Enter submitting immediately. I'll also cancel in-flight requests when the input changes using context cancellation to avoid showing stale results.

Now I'm sketching out the package structure with a root command that launches the TUI by default, and subcommands for one-off translation from arguments or stdin. I'm thinking through the engine abstraction and how to make it feel like a Marvin-style system—defining typed request/response structs, building LLM prompts from templates with JSON schemas, and unmarshaling structured output. The `TranslateResult` struct would include the translation, detected source language, alternatives, confidence, and grammar notes. For the built-in prompts, I'm planning a system message that instructs the model to be concise and faithful, detect the source language if needed, return structured JSON with alternatives and brief contextual notes, and handle typos gracefully by inferring intent. I'm also sketching out recommended models for the init wizard, starting with Claude Sonnet as a balanced default.

Writing config schema... I'll build it as a standalone Go module in the tries directory with `go build -o translate`, noting that it can graduate into dotfiles later as a binary in `~/.dotfiles/bin/` or via `go install`. Before diving into implementation, I should run a focused design workflow to pressure-test the Go architecture—validating library choices (sqlite driver for static binaries with FTS5, config/TOML, XDG paths, TUI components, huh for the wizard), the Bubble Tea concurrency pattern for live-debounce with in-flight cancellation and streaming display, and the engine interface design with fallback chaining and structured output.

I'm fairly confident about most of the Go libraries (huh exists via Charm, bubbles has textarea, modernc.org/sqlite supports FTS5), but I should do a couple of quick web checks on the riskiest assumptions—particularly whether modernc's FTS5 support is solid and the Google free endpoint reliability—rather than rely on agent knowledge cutoff. I'll launch a compact workflow with parallel design agents each tackling the architecture from different angles (TUI/UX concurrency, engine/provider abstraction, config/storage/CLI ergonomics) plus web-verification agents for the load-bearing library facts, then synthesize the results. I'm going to use a Workflow instead—it aligns with both the plan-mode instructions and ultracode's guidance to delegate the design work. I'll structure it with three phases: verification of key Go libraries and APIs, parallel design agents tackling the TUI/concurrency, engine/providers, and config/storage layers, and a critique phase to pressure-test the architecture. Let me draft the workflow script with self-contained prompts for each agent since they won't have access to my context.

Writing workflow script... Defining the agent prompts and workflow structure... I'm setting up the workflow structure with design as a gating step after verification—since designs need to reference verified facts—and then critique comes after all designs are complete. Now I'll write out the script.
</details></think>

<summary>Dynamic workflow "Verify Go/Charm libraries and endpoints, then design the translate CLI/TUI architecture" completed</summary>
<result>{"verified":["Research complete. Returning findings.\n\n---\n\n# Verified Go library facts\n\n## 1. SQLite in a pure-Go static binary (modernc + FTS5)\n- **modernc.org/sqlite is CGO-free and ships FTS5 compiled in by default.** No build tag, no loadable extension. The `modernc.org/sqlite/lib` package exposes `FTS5_*` constants, and the gosqlite wrapper states verbatim: *\"FTS5 is compiled into modernc.org/sqlite/lib … by default — no extension to load.\"* Current builds bundle SQLite ~3.53.2 (which includes FTS5 in core).\n- Contrast: `mattn/go-sqlite3` (CGO) requires `go build -tags \"fts5\"` and a C toolchain — avoid it for a single static cross-compiled binary.\n- **Recommended driver:**\n  ```go\n  import (\n      \"database/sql\"\n      _ \"modernc.org/sqlite\" // driver name is \"sqlite\" (NOT \"sqlite3\")\n  )\n  db, _ := sql.Open(\"sqlite\", \"file:history.db?_pragma=journal_mode(WAL)\")\n  db.Exec(`CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE history_fts USING fts5(source, translation, notes)`)\n  ```\n- **Verify at runtime** (cheap self-test on first open, so you can fall back gracefully): `SELECT sqlite_compileoption_used('ENABLE_FTS5')` returns `1`. This is worth doing since FTS5-in-modernc is well-supported but version-dependent — pin the module version in go.mod.\n- **Fallback if FTS5 ever fails:** plain `LIKE`/`instr` search on a normal table (adequate for a personal history table of thousands of rows). No need for bbolt.\n- Uncertainty flag: modernc FTS5 has been solid for years, but I confirmed it via the wrapper package docs and the exposed lib constants rather than an explicit line in the main README — the runtime `sqlite_compileoption_used` check removes all doubt.\n\n## 2. SQLite vs JSONL for prototype history — recommendation\n- **For v1, prefer a JSONL append file + in-memory filter/fuzzy search.** Translation history is append-mostly, low-volume (hundreds–thousands of entries), and a full file scan on startup is microseconds. This eliminates a schema, migrations, FTS5 tokenizer choices, and the WAL/locking surface — big simplicity win for a prototype.\n  - Sketch: append one `TranslateResult` JSON object per line to `~/.local/share/translate/history.jsonl`; on TUI open, read all lines into `[]TranslateResult`; fuzzy-rank with `sahilm/fuzzy` (in-memory, same lib Bubbles uses).\n- **\"Last language pair\" is state, not history** — put it in a tiny `state.json`/`state.toml` under `XDG_STATE_HOME`, don't stand up a DB for it.\n- **Choose sqlite/FTS5 only if** you want ranked substring/prefix search over a large corpus or SQL querying later. Since modernc+FTS5 is genuinely zero-friction (static, cgo-free), graduating to it later is cheap.\n- **Actionable:** define a `Store` interface (`Add(TranslateResult)`, `Search(q) []TranslateResult`, `LastPair()`) and back it with JSONL in v1; swap to sqlite behind the same interface if/when it graduates into dotfiles. This keeps the locked \"sqlite w/ FTS\" decision as a drop-in upgrade, not a v1 blocker.\n\n## 3. XDG paths on macOS — IMPORTANT CORRECTION to the locked assumption\n- **`github.com/adrg/xdg` does NOT default to `~/.config` on macOS.** Its macOS defaults are:\n  - `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` → `~/Library/Application Support`\n  - `XDG_DATA_HOME`  → `~/Library/Application Support`\n  - `XDG_STATE_HOME` → `~/Library/Application Support`\n  - `XDG_CACHE_HOME` → `~/Library/Caches`\n- It **does honor the env vars** if set (they take precedence over the macOS defaults). So `~/.config` on macOS happens *only if* `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is exported.\n- Stdlib `os.UserConfigDir()` **also** returns `~/Library/Application Support` on macOS — so neither adrg nor stdlib gives `~/.config` on macOS by default.\n- **The project's stated convention (`~/.config/translate/`, `~/.local/share/…`, `~/.local/state/…`) is Linux-XDG semantics, which macOS libraries deliberately do not follow.** To honor it on macOS you must not rely on defaults.\n- **Recommendation:** write a tiny resolver that mirrors freedesktop semantics on all platforms (this is what tools like Neovim/lazygit-style `~/.config` users expect):\n  ```go\n  func configHome() string {\n      if v := os.Getenv(\"XDG_CONFIG_HOME\"); v != \"\" { return v }\n      h, _ := os.UserHomeDir()\n      return filepath.Join(h, \".config\") // ~/.config on macOS AND Linux\n  }\n  // same pattern: XDG_DATA_HOME -&gt; ~/.local/share, XDG_STATE_HOME -&gt; ~/.local/state\n  ```\n  Then `filepath.Join(configHome(), \"translate\", \"config.toml\")`, etc., and `os.MkdirAll` the parent on first run.\n- If you still want adrg/xdg's ergonomics (its `ConfigFile`, `SearchConfigFile`, `UserDirs`, `Reload()` are nice), you can force the convention by exporting the env vars before its `init`, or by using it only on Linux and your own resolver on macOS — but for a `~/.config`-convention tool, the ~10-line manual resolver is simpler and has no surprising platform branch. adrg/xdg API for reference: `xdg.ConfigFile(\"translate/config.toml\")`, `xdg.DataFile(...)`, `xdg.StateFile(...)`, `xdg.SearchConfigFile(...)`, vars `xdg.ConfigHome`/`xdg.DataHome`/`xdg.StateHome`.\n\n## 4. TOML library — recommendation\n- **Use `github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2`.** Idiomatic default in 2026: fastest, actively maintained, targets TOML 1.0/1.1, clean `encoding/json`-style `Marshal`/`Unmarshal` with `toml:\"...\"` struct tags, and correctly round-trips nested tables and **arrays-of-tables (`[[provider]]`)** in both directions — exactly your config shape.\n  ```go\n  import \"github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2\"\n  type Provider struct { Name, BaseURL, Model string `toml:\"...\"` }\n  type Config struct {\n      Engine    string     `toml:\"engine\"`\n      Providers []Provider `toml:\"provider\"` // emits/reads [[provider]]\n  }\n  b, _ := toml.Marshal(cfg)\n  _ = toml.Unmarshal(data, &amp;cfg)\n  ```\n- **Avoid `github.com/pelletier/go-toml` v1** — deprecated in favor of v2.\n- **`github.com/BurntSushi/toml`** is fine and stable for *decoding* `[[provider]]` into `[]Provider`, but its encoder has quirks around top-level arrays-of-tables and is less ergonomic for read-modify-write. Since `translate init` will both write and read config, go-toml/v2 is the better fit.\n\n---\n\n### Final recommendations (import paths)\n- **SQLite driver:** `modernc.org/sqlite` (cgo-free, driver name `\"sqlite\"`, FTS5 built in) — but **for v1 use JSONL history** (`~/.local/share/translate/history.jsonl`) + in-memory fuzzy (`github.com/sahilm/fuzzy`) behind a `Store` interface; keep modernc+FTS5 as the drop-in upgrade.\n- **XDG paths:** roll a ~10-line `XDG_*`-with-`~/.config` fallback resolver (do **not** rely on `github.com/adrg/xdg` or `os.UserConfigDir` defaults — both map to `~/Library/Application Support` on macOS, breaking your `~/.config` convention).\n- **TOML:** `github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2` for marshal+unmarshal of the nested-table / `[[provider]]` config.\n\nSources: [modernc.org/sqlite](https://pkg.go.dev/modernc.org/sqlite), [modernc.org/sqlite/lib (FTS5 constants)](https://pkg.go.dev/modernc.org/sqlite/lib), [gosqlite fts wrapper (FTS5 default)](https://pkg.go.dev/gosqlite.org%40v0.10.0/fts), [mattn/go-sqlite3 FTS5 build-tag contrast](https://github.com/mattn/go-sqlite3/issues/340), [adrg/xdg README](https://github.com/adrg/xdg), [SQLite FTS5 docs](https://www.sqlite.org/fts5.html), [pelletier/go-toml/v2](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2), [BurntSushi/toml](https://github.com/BurntSushi/toml).","All facts confirmed. Here is the verified research report.\n\n---\n\n# Charm TUI stack — verified current patterns (July 2026)\n\n## ⚠️ Headline finding: the stack moved to v2 + new module paths (Feb 2026)\n\nCharm shipped the **v2 family out of beta on ~2026-02-23** and moved to a **`charm.land/*/v2` vanity module path** (not `github.com/charmbracelet/...`). For a greenfield project in July 2026 this is the current idiomatic stack. Pin these:\n\n| Lib | Import path | Version | Role |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Bubble Tea

So `~/.config` on macOS happens *only if* `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is exported.\n- Stdlib `os.UserConfigDir()` **also** returns `~/Library/Application Support` on macOS — so neither adrg nor stdlib gives `~/.config` on macOS by default.\n- **The project's stated convention (`~/.config/translate/`, `~/.local/share/…`, `~/.local/state/…`) is Linux-XDG semantics, which macOS libraries deliberately do not follow.** To honor it on macOS you must not rely on defaults.\n- **Recommendation:** write a tiny resolver that mirrors freedesktop semantics on all platforms (this is what tools like Neovim/lazygit-style `~/.config` users expect):\n  ```go\n  func configHome() string {\n      if v := os.Getenv(\"XDG_CONFIG_HOME\"); v != \"\" { return v }\n      h, _ := os.UserHomeDir()\n      return filepath.Join(h, \".config\") // ~/.config on macOS AND Linux\n  }\n  // same pattern: XDG_DATA_HOME -> ~/.local/share, XDG_STATE_HOME -> ~/.local/state\n  ```\n  Then `filepath.Join(configHome(), \"translate\", \"config.toml\")`, etc., and `os.MkdirAll` the parent on first run.\n- If you still want adrg/xdg's ergonomics (its `ConfigFile`, `SearchConfigFile`, `UserDirs`, `Reload()` are nice), you can force the convention by exporting the env vars before its `init`, or by using it only on Linux and your own resolver on macOS — but for a `~/.config`-convention tool, the ~10-line manual resolver is simpler and has no surprising platform branch. adrg/xdg API for reference: `xdg.ConfigFile(\"translate/config.toml\")`, `xdg.DataFile(...)`, `xdg.StateFile(...)`, `xdg.SearchConfigFile(...)`, vars `xdg.ConfigHome`/`xdg.DataHome`/`xdg.StateHome`.\n\n## 4. TOML library — recommendation\n- **Use `github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2`.** Idiomatic default in 2026: fastest, actively maintained, targets TOML 1.0/1.1, clean `encoding/json`-style `Marshal`/`Unmarshal` with `toml:\"...\"` struct tags, and correctly round-trips nested tables and **arrays-of-tables (`[[provider]]`)** in both directions — exactly your config shape.\n  ```go\n  import \"github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2\"\n  type Provider struct { Name, BaseURL, Model string `toml:\"...\"` }\n  type Config struct {\n      Engine    string     `toml:\"engine\"`\n      Providers []Provider `toml:\"provider\"` // emits/reads [[provider]]\n  }\n  b, _ := toml.Marshal(cfg)\n  _ = toml.Unmarshal(data, &cfg)\n  ```\n- **Avoid `github.com/pelletier/go-toml` v1** — deprecated in favor of v2.\n- **`github.com/BurntSushi/toml`** is fine and stable for *decoding* `[[provider]]` into `[]Provider`, but its encoder has quirks around top-level arrays-of-tables and is less ergonomic for read-modify-write. Since `translate init` will both write and read config, go-toml/v2 is the better fit.\n\n---\n\n### Final recommendations (import paths)\n- **SQLite driver:** `modernc.org/sqlite` (cgo-free, driver name `\"sqlite\"`, FTS5 built in) — but **for v1 use JSONL history** (`~/.local/share/translate/history.jsonl`) + in-memory fuzzy (`github.com/sahilm/fuzzy`) behind a `Store` interface; keep modernc+FTS5 as the drop-in upgrade.\n- **XDG paths:** roll a ~10-line `XDG_*`-with-`~/.config` fallback resolver (do **not** rely on `github.com/adrg/xdg` or `os.UserConfigDir` defaults — both map to `~/Library/Application Support` on macOS, breaking your `~/.config` convention).\n- **TOML:** `github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2` for marshal+unmarshal of the nested-table / `[[provider]]` config.\n\nSources: [modernc.org/sqlite](https://pkg.go.dev/modernc.org/sqlite), [modernc.org/sqlite/lib (FTS5 constants)](https://pkg.go.dev/modernc.org/sqlite/lib), [gosqlite fts wrapper (FTS5 default)](https://pkg.go.dev/gosqlite.org%40v0.10.0/fts), [mattn/go-sqlite3 FTS5 build-tag contrast](https://github.com/mattn/go-sqlite3/issues/340), [adrg/xdg README](https://github.com/adrg/xdg), [SQLite FTS5 docs](https://www.sqlite.org/fts5.html), [pelletier/go-toml/v2](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2), [BurntSushi/toml](https://github.com/BurntSushi/toml).",
8	      "All facts confirmed. Here is the verified research report.\n\n---\n\n# Charm TUI stack — verified current patterns (July 2026)\n\n## ⚠️ Headline finding: the stack moved to v2 + new module paths (Feb 2026)\n\nCharm shipped the **v2 family out of beta on ~2026-02-23** and moved to a **`charm.land/*/v2` vanity module path** (not `github.com/charmbracelet/...`). For a greenfield project in July 2026 this is the current idiomatic stack. Pin these:\n\n| Lib | Import path | Version | Role |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Bubble Tea | `charm.land/bubbletea/v2` | v2.0.8 | Elm runtime (Model/Update/View, Cmd/Msg) |\n| Bubbles | `charm.land/bubbles/v2` | v2.0.x | components (subpackages below) |\n| Lip Gloss | `charm.land/lipgloss/v2` | v2.0.5 | styling + layout |\n| huh | `charm.land/huh/v2` | v2.0.3 | forms; built on tea v2 + lipgloss v2 |\n\nRequires **Go 1.25**. cobra is unaffected (still `github.com/spf13/cobra`).\n\n**Biggest caveat for you:** almost all tutorials, blog posts, and — critically — LLM-generated code (including the copilot-proxy models you plan to call) default to **v1** (`github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea`, `View() string`, `tea.KeyMsg`). v1 code will **not compile against v2**. Be explicit in prompts/CLAUDE.md that this is a v2 `charm.land/*` project. v1 is still maintained (bubbletea v1.3.10) if you'd rather ride the better-documented path, but then use v1 paths consistently.\n\n### v2 breaking changes that matter here\n- `View() tea.View` (was `string`) → wrap with `tea.NewView(\"...\")`.\n- Keys: `tea.KeyPressMsg` (was `tea.KeyMsg`); fields `.Code`, `.Text` (string, was `.Runes`), `.Mod` (was `.Alt`). `.String()` still works for matching, and `bubbles/v2/key` still works.\n- Cursor is declarative via the `tea.View.Cursor` field (imperative cursor cmds removed).\n- `tea.RequestWindowSize` (renamed from `tea.WindowSize`). `tea.WindowSizeMsg` unchanged.\n- `Init() tea.Cmd` and `Update(tea.Msg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd)` **unchanged**. `tea.NewProgram(m, tea.WithAltScreen()).Run()`, `tea.Batch`, `tea.Sequence`, `tea.Tick`, `tea.Quit` **unchanged**.\n\n## 1. Components — all present\n\n**Bubbles v2** subpackages (import `charm.land/bubbles/v2/<pkg>`): `textarea`, `textinput`, `viewport`, `list`, `spinner`, `help`, `key`, plus `table`, `progress`, `paginator`, `cursor`, `filepicker`, `timer`, `stopwatch`. Everything you listed exists.\n\n**huh v2** field constructors: `NewInput`, `NewText`, `NewSelect[T]`, `NewMultiSelect[T]`, `NewConfirm`, grouped via `NewGroup`/`NewForm`. Runs **both ways**:\n- Standalone wizard: `err := form.Run()` (blocking) — ideal for `translate init`.\n- Embedded: `huh.Form` **is a `tea.Model`** — call `form.Init/Update/View`, check `m.form.State == huh.StateCompleted`. In a v2 parent, `form.View()` returns a string, so wrap it: `return tea.NewView(m.form.View())`.\n\nFor the probing init wizard, run huh **standalone** (`form.Run()`) and do the provider HTTP probes either before the form (show results as `Description()` on options) or between groups — simplest is standalone, not embedded.\n\n## 2. Debounce + cancel + stream skeleton (the core)\n\nAll three keyed off **one monotonic `seq` counter**. Every keystroke bumps `seq`, which (a) invalidates the pending debounce tick, (b) is the cancel signal for the in-flight request, and (c) stamps every stream msg so stale/cancelled chunks are dropped in O(1). This is exactly the pattern in Charm's official `examples/debounce` (tag counter + `tea.Tick`) fused with `examples/realtime` (self-resubscribing channel reader).\n\n```go\nimport (\n    \"context\"; \"time\"\n    tea \"charm.land/bubbletea/v2\"\n    \"charm.land/bubbles/v2/textarea\"\n    \"charm.land/bubbles/v2/viewport\"\n)\n\ntype debounceMsg  struct{ seq int }\ntype chunkMsg      struct{ seq int; text string }\ntype doneMsg       struct{ seq int; res TranslateResult; err error }\n\ntype model struct {\n    ta         textarea.Model\n    vp         viewport.Model\n    seq        int                 // generation / request id\n    cancel     context.CancelFunc  // cancels in-flight engine call\n    stream     chan chunkMsg       // engine -> UI\n    debounceOn bool\n    result     string\n}\n\nfunc (m model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {\n    switch msg := msg.(type) {\n\n    case tea.KeyPressMsg:\n        if msg.String() == \"enter\" {           // immediate translate\n            return m.launch()                  // (bumps seq, cancels, kicks goroutine)\n        }\n        var cmd tea.Cmd\n        m.ta, cmd = m.ta.Update(msg)\n        if m.debounceOn {\n            m.seq++                             // (a) invalidate pending tick\n            if m.cancel != nil { m.cancel() }   // (b) cancel in-flight\n            return m, tea.Batch(cmd, arm(m.seq))\n        }\n        return m, cmd\n\n    case debounceMsg:                           // 400ms of idle elapsed?\n        if msg.seq != m.seq { return m, nil }   // newer keystroke arrived -> ignore\n        return m.launch()\n\n    case chunkMsg:\n        if msg.seq != m.seq { return m, nil }   // (c) stale/cancelled -> drop\n        m.result += msg.text\n        m.vp.SetContent(m.result)\n        return m, m.waitChunk(m.seq)            // re-subscribe for next token\n\n    case doneMsg:\n        if msg.seq != m.seq { return m, nil }\n        // render final structured TranslateResult (alternatives, notes, confidence)\n        return m, nil\n    }\n    return m, nil\n}\n\nfunc arm(seq int) tea.Cmd {                     // debounce timer\n    return tea.Tick(400*time.Millisecond, func(time.Time) tea.Msg {\n        return debounceMsg{seq: seq}\n    })\n}\n\n// launch: bump seq, make a fresh context+channel, start streaming goroutine.\nfunc (m model) launch() (tea.Model, tea.Cmd) {\n    m.seq++\n    if m.cancel != nil { m.cancel() }\n    ctx, cancel := context.WithCancel(context.Background())\n    ch := make(chan chunkMsg, 32)\n    m.cancel, m.stream, m.result = cancel, ch, \"\"\n    seq, text := m.seq, m.ta.Value()\n\n    go func() {                                 // engine respects ctx; OpenAI SSE or google call\n        defer close(ch)\n        engine.Stream(ctx, text, func(tok string) {\n            select {\n            case ch <- chunkMsg{seq: seq, text: tok}:\n            case <-ctx.Done():                  // cancelled: stop feeding\n            }\n        })\n    }()\n    return m, m.waitChunk(seq)\n}\n\nfunc (m model) waitChunk(seq int) tea.Cmd {     // self-resubscribing reader (realtime pattern)\n    ch := m.stream\n    return func() tea.Msg {\n        c, ok := <-ch\n        if !ok { return doneMsg{seq: seq} }     // channel closed = stream finished\n        return c\n    }\n}\n```\n\nNotes / correctness:\n- **`seq` is the single source of truth.** You do not even need to check `ctx` in the UI thread — the `msg.seq != m.seq` guard makes cancelled results unrenderable. `ctx` cancellation is purely to stop wasted network/LLM work.\n- `tea.Tick` fires exactly once per call, so one armed tick per keystroke; the guard collapses the storm to the last one. This is the sanctioned Charm idiom (their comment: \"if the tag in the Msg matches current tag… otherwise we simply ignore the inbound message\").\n- Non-streaming engines (google free API, dictionary) just skip `chunkMsg` and return a single `doneMsg{seq, res}` from a plain `tea.Cmd`.\n- Buffer the channel (e.g. 32) so a fast LLM stream doesn't block on a slow render frame.\n\n## 3. Two-pane responsive layout (Lip Gloss v2)\n\n`NewStyle`, `JoinHorizontal`, `JoinVertical`, `Place`, `Width/Height`, `Border` all survive into v2. Recompute widths on `tea.WindowSizeMsg`; compose with joins.\n\n```go\nimport lg \"charm.land/lipgloss/v2\"\n\ncase tea.WindowSizeMsg:\n    m.width, m.height = msg.Width, msg.Height\n    footerH := 1\n    leftW  := msg.Width * 2 / 3\n    rightW := msg.Width - leftW\n    bodyH  := msg.Height - footerH\n    inputH := 4\n    m.ta.SetWidth(leftW-2);  m.ta.SetHeight(inputH)\n    m.vp.Width = leftW - 2;  m.vp.Height = bodyH - inputH - 2\n    m.hist.SetSize(rightW-2, bodyH)\n\nfunc (m model) View() tea.View {\n    left := lg.JoinVertical(lg.Left,\n        boxStyle.Render(m.ta.View()),\n        boxStyle.Render(m.vp.View()))\n    right  := boxStyle.Width(m.rightW).Render(m.hist.View())\n    body   := lg.JoinHorizontal(lg.Top, left, right)\n    footer := footerStyle.Render(m.help.View(m.keys))\n    return tea.NewView(lg.JoinVertical(lg.Left, body, footer))\n}\n```\n\nColor in v2: no global renderer. Use `lg.Color(\"#7D56F4\")` / ANSI codes; for light/dark adaptivity handle `tea.BackgroundColorMsg` (send `tea.RequestBackgroundColor`, use `msg.IsDark()`) inside the TUI, or `lipgloss.HasDarkBackground(os.Stdin, os.Stdout)` standalone. A `compat` package offers v1-style `AdaptiveColor` but Charm explicitly discourages it for new code.\n\n## 4. stdin-pipe vs TTY gotcha — real, handle it\n\nBubble Tea takes over the TTY via a cancelreader; if stdin is a pipe the interactive loop gets immediate EOF/garbage. **Branch before launching.** Use `golang.org/x/term.IsTerminal(int(os.Stdin.Fd()))` (preferred over `mattn/go-isatty`; or `os.Stdin.Stat()` + `os.ModeCharDevice`).\n\n```go\nfunc run(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {\n    switch {\n    case len(args) > 0:                                   // translate \"text\"\n        return oneShot(strings.Join(args, \" \"))\n    case !term.IsTerminal(int(os.Stdin.Fd())):            // echo x | translate\n        b, _ := io.ReadAll(os.Stdin)\n        return oneShot(strings.TrimSpace(string(b)))\n    default:                                              // bare, interactive TTY\n        _, err := tea.NewProgram(newModel(), tea.WithAltScreen()).Run()\n        return err\n    }\n}\n```\n\nAlso gate color/ANSI on **stdout** being a TTY for the one-shot path (so `translate x | pcopy` stays clean) — Lip Gloss v2 auto-downsamples when you emit via its writer helpers or when output isn't a TTY. cobra's \"did you mean\" suggestions are on by default (`SuggestionsMinimumDistance`), satisfying the subcommand-typo requirement; language-name fuzzing (chinees→zh) is your own resolver, not cobra.\n\n---\n\n### Quick recommendation summary\n- Adopt the **v2 `charm.land/*` stack** (tea 2.0.8, bubbles 2.0.x, lipgloss 2.0.5, huh 2.0.3, Go 1.25); document it loudly so v1-trained code-gen doesn't poison the build.\n- **One `seq` counter** unifies debounce-ignore, cancel, and stale-stream-drop — mirror Charm's `debounce` + `realtime` examples. Keep `context.Context`/`cancel` on the model only to stop wasted engine work.\n- huh: **standalone `form.Run()`** for `translate init`; embed-as-`tea.Model` only if you later fold it into the TUI.\n- Layout via `JoinHorizontal/JoinVertical` recomputed on `WindowSizeMsg`; `View()` returns `tea.NewView(...)`.\n- Detect `!IsTerminal(stdin)` (or args present) → one-shot; else TUI.\n\n### Sources\n- https://charm.land/blog/v2/ — v2 out-of-beta announcement (2026-02-23)\n- https://github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/blob/main/UPGRADE_GUIDE_V2.md — v2 breaking changes (`View() tea.View`, `KeyPressMsg`, `RequestWindowSize`)\n- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/main/examples/debounce/main.go — canonical tag/seq + `tea.Tick` debounce\n- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea/main/examples/realtime/main.go — self-resubscribing channel-reader Cmd (streaming)\n- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/bubbles/main/go.mod — `module charm.land/bubbles/v2`, requires tea v2.0.8, lipgloss v2.0.5\n- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/lipgloss/main/go.mod — `module charm.land/lipgloss/v2`\n- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/charmbracelet/huh/main/go.mod — `module charm.land/huh/v2`, on tea v2 + lipgloss v2\n- https://pkg.go.dev/charm.land/huh/v2 — huh v2 API (Form is a tea.Model, StateCompleted)\n- https://golang.org/x/term — `IsTerminal(int(fd))`",
9	      "Verified. Final recommendations below.\n\n## 1. Google unofficial endpoint — CONFIRMED, use as free-path default\n`GET https://translate.googleapis.com/translate_a/single?client=gtx&sl=<auto|src>&tl=<tgt>&dt=t&q=<urlenc>`\n\nLive response for `sl=auto&tl=es&q=hello world` (with `dt=t&dt=bd&dt=at`):\n```\n[[[\"Hola Mundo\",\"hello world\",null,null,10]],null,\"en\",null,null,\n [[\"hello world\",null,[[\"Hola Mundo\",null,true,false,[10]],[\"hola mundial\",null,true,false,[8]]],[[0,11]],\"hello world\",0,0]],\n 0.76348495,[],[[\"en\"],null,[0.76348495],[\"en\"]]]\n```\nParsing (nested-array positions are stable across the projects that use this):\n- **Full translation** = concatenate `data[0][i][0]` for all i (segment 0 = translated chunk, segment 1 = original chunk). Never assume one segment — long text splits into many.\n- **Detected source lang** = `data[2]` (`\"en\"`). Confidence at `data[8][2][0]` (~0.76).\n- **`dt` params are additive and each adds a top-level slot**: `dt=t` (translation, index 0), `dt=bd` = **b**ilingual **d**ictionary block at `data[1]` (per-POS: `[posLabel, [terms...], [[term, [reverseTranslations], score, freq]...], ...]`) — only populated for single words/lemmas, `null` for phrases like \"hello world\"; `dt=at` = **a**lternate translations at `data[5]`. You can pass multiple `dt=` in one call.\n- Recommendation: request `client=gtx&dt=t&dt=bd&dt=at` always; parse index 0 (required), 2 (detect), and opportunistically 1/5 to enrich the Marvin-lite `Alternatives[]`.\n\nCaveats: undocumented/unofficial, can break without notice; community reports **HTTP 429** after bursts and occasional 403/captcha. Treat as best-effort: single request per debounce (the 400ms debounce + cancel-in-flight already throttles well), add exponential backoff+jitter on 429, and fall through to the next engine on any non-200. Set a browser-ish `User-Agent`; `client=gtx` avoids the `tk` token requirement.\n\nAlternative free option — **MyMemory** (CONFIRMED, good secondary): `GET https://api.mymemory.translated.net/get?q=<text>&langpair=en|es`\n```json\n{\"responseData\":{\"translatedText\":\"Hola Mundo\",\"match\":1},\"responseStatus\":200,\n \"quotaFinished\":false,\"matches\":[{\"translation\":\"...\",\"quality\":\"...\",\"match\":0.9,...}]}\n```\nSimple flat shape (`responseData.translatedText`, `responseStatus`, `matches[]` for alternatives). Limits: **5,000 chars/day anonymous**, 50,000/day if you add `&de=email@x`; **500 bytes max per `q`**. No auto-detect of source (needs explicit langpair). Recommendation: keep Google unofficial as primary free path (has detect + no daily cap), wire MyMemory as an easy fallback since its shape is trivial. Skip public LibreTranslate instances for v1 — the public ones are flaky/require keys now.\n\n## 2. Bing/Microsoft — DEFER\nNo documented keyless path. The community `https://www.bing.com/ttranslatev3` route requires scraping transient `IG`/`IID`/`token`/`key` from the translator web page first, then POSTing — fragile, ToS-risky, breaks often. The only supported Microsoft path is Azure Translator (`api.cognitive.microsofttranslator.com`, requires Azure subscription key). Recommendation: **do not include Bing in v1**; the LLM chain + Google + MyMemory cover the free need. Leave an engine interface seam so an Azure-key Bing engine can drop in later.\n\n## 3. Dictionary — use dictionaryapi.dev for v1, local wordlist+Levenshtein for fuzzy\nFree Dictionary API (CONFIRMED): `GET https://api.dictionaryapi.dev/api/v2/entries/en/<word>` → array of entries:\n```\nword, phonetic, phonetics[]{text,audio}, meanings[]{ partOfSpeech,\n  definitions[]{definition, example?, synonyms[], antonyms[]}, synonyms[], antonyms[] },\nlicense{name,url}, sourceUrls[]\n```\nOn miss it returns **HTTP 404** (confirmed for `helllo`) with a `{title,message,resolution}` body — **no suggestions/nearest-word field**. So the API cannot power fuzzy itself.\n\nFuzzy strategy recommendation: on 404, resolve nearest headword **locally** — ship a bundled English wordlist (e.g. SCOWL/`words` ~50–100k, or the dictionaryapi word set) and rank candidates by Levenshtein/Damerau distance (Go: `github.com/agnivade/levenshtein` or `agext/levenshtein`), optionally pre-filter by a trigram/prefix index for speed, then re-query the API with the top suggestion (mirrors the CLI's cobra \"did you mean\" UX). This keeps it offline-capable and deterministic.\n\nWiktionary REST (`https://en.wiktionary.org/api/rest_v1/page/definition/<word>`) — object keyed by language code (`\"en\"`, `\"fr\"`, …), each value an array of `{partOfSpeech, language, definitions[]{definition, parsedExamples[], examples[]}}`. It's **multi-language** (good later) but definitions contain HTML markup needing stripping, and it's heavier to parse. Recommendation: **v1 = dictionaryapi.dev** (clean JSON, English, synonyms/antonyms/phonetics/audio out of the box); keep Wiktionary REST as a documented option for multi-language dictionary in a later version.\n\n## 4. Offline language detection in Go — recommend whatlanggo for the prototype\n- `github.com/abadojack/whatlanggo` — light, pure-Go, fast, no large model download, trivial API (`whatlang.Detect(text)` → lang + script + confidence). Best fit for a prototype and for the \"auto\" source when Google is offline.\n- `github.com/pemistahl/lingua-go` — most accurate on short/single-word/mixed text, but heavy (large embedded n-gram models, bigger binary, slower init) — matters for a \"single static binary\" goal.\n\nRecommendation: **start with whatlanggo** (`github.com/abadojack/whatlanggo`) for binary size + speed; note lingua-go as an upgrade path behind the same internal `Detector` interface if short-text accuracy proves insufficient. In practice detection is mostly a fallback since Google returns `data[2]` and the LLM returns `DetectedSource` in the structured result — so keeping detection light is the right call.\n\nNote: I did not write a plan file — per instructions these findings are returned directly as my output.\n\nSources: [Free Dictionary API](https://dictionaryapi.dev/), [Wiktionary REST](https://en.wiktionary.org/api/rest_v1/), [MyMemory limits](https://mymemory.translated.net/doc/usagelimits.php), [lingua-go](https://github.com/pemistahl/lingua-go), [whatlanggo](https://github.com/abadojack/whatlanggo), [Azure Translator v3](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/translator/text-translation/reference/v3/translate)."
